name: Technical Proof Anchor Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Choosing demonstrator-first proves feasibility without regulatory risk, but production-oriented proof claims higher credibility at cost of 6+ additional months and certification friction. Systemic: Demonstrator-first enables phase-two public release and media narrative by month 16, accelerating coalition pressure; production-path delays AltID influence window. Strategic: Demonstrator credibility depends on external security review and standards alignment (FIDO2, OpenID Connect); insufficient review weakens procurement-language advocacy.
options: Non-production demonstrators using GrapheneOS and open standards, published with independent security audit to establish technical feasibility without regulatory friction
Hybrid prototype seeking limited MitID operator collaboration to test fallback authentication against live identity-provider simulations, accepting 8-week review cycles
Production-grade certification pathway pursuing formal Danish eIDAS2 compliance, deferring all deliverables 6+ months but claiming full regulatory legitimacy
review: Controls Technical Credibility vs. Timeline Urgency. Weakness: The options do not address the risk that even certified demonstrators may be dismissed by procurement bodies as 'not production-ready' unless explicitly positioned as evidence of architectural feasibility rather than product-ready software.

name: Procurement Language Framing
consequences: Immediate: Resilience-first framing (supplier concentration, continuity risk, service availability) appeals to contingency planners and avoids ideological resistance; sovereignty framing triggers nationalist pushback but resonates with politicians. Systemic: Resilience language increases support from IT operations and business-continuity stakeholders; sovereignty language concentrates support among digital-independence advocates and may alienate pragmatists. Strategic: Whichever framing dominates phase-three procurement language determines whether fallback authentication becomes a 'continuity requirement' or a 'strategic autonomy feature,' affecting implementation urgency and budget priority.
options: Lead with supplier-concentration and service-continuity risk narrative, positioning platform neutrality as essential redundancy and business-continuity insurance
Emphasize digital sovereignty, democratic accountability, and reduced foreign vendor influence, framing fallback access as a public-sector right
Combine resilience and standards-alignment messaging, situating platform-neutral requirements within EU eIDAS2 interoperability and portability mandates, avoiding nationalist framing
review: Controls Message Authority vs. Stakeholder Coalition. Weakness: The options assume each framing can be deployed independently, but procurement bodies typically require multiple justifications in sequence; the options do not specify fallback narratives if the primary frame is rejected by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.

name: Institutional Engagement Sequencing
consequences: Immediate: Early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen (phase one) establishes legitimacy but risks premature rejection if feasibility report is incomplete; delayed engagement until phase two allows stronger technical evidence but may miss procurement-planning windows. Systemic: Early engagement can influence AltID architecture at design stage; late engagement forces advocacy against entrenched decisions and requires standards/EU pressure as leverage. Strategic: Failure to engage with MitID operator directly may leave critical technical assumptions unvalidated; priority sequencing (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen → MitID operator → Folketinget committee) versus parallel outreach affects influence timing and resource allocation.
options: Prioritize formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator during phase one (months 1–8) using preliminary feasibility findings, risking early rejection but maximizing design-stage influence

Sequence engagement conservatively: complete full technical demonstrators and security review in phase two before approaching Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and procurement stakeholders in phase three (months 17–24)

Conduct parallel low-visibility engagement with sympathetic Folketinget committee members and procurement experts during phase one, building political coalition before formal institutional approach, deferring official Digitaliseringsstyrelsen contact to phase two
review: Controls Influence Timing vs. Evidence Maturity. Weakness: None of the options address the scenario in which Digitaliseringsstyrelsen is actively uninterested in platform-neutrality and prefers to extend the existing Apple/Google relationship; the options assume receptiveness and do not specify pivot strategies or alternative procurement levers.

name: Fallback Authentication Technology Scope
consequences: Immediate: Mobile-centric fallback (using privacy-respecting Android forks) remains tethered to mobile OS ecosystem and does not truly eliminate platform dependence; browser-based fallback (FIDO2 security keys, WebAuthn) requires users to maintain hardware tokens and infrastructure support. Systemic: Mobile fallback maintains high usability but duplicates the problem it aims to solve (OS-level lock-in); browser-fallback reduces OS lock-in but creates UX burden and requires broader IT infrastructure modernization. Strategic: Choosing mobile-centric limits sovereign autonomy claims; choosing hardware-token-based proves deeper platform independence but may be rejected as inconvenient by public-sector procurement bodies prioritizing citizen ease-of-use over vendor neutrality.
options: Develop mobile-first fallback using GrapheneOS or comparable privacy-respecting Android derivative, emphasizing open-source alternatives while accepting continued dependence on mobile OS ecosystems

Design browser-based and hardware-token-centric fallback path (FIDO2/U2F, WebAuthn, open-source authenticator apps), eliminating exclusive reliance on mobile OS vendors but requiring infrastructure and UX investment
Create dual-path fallback: mobile option for convenience (GrapheneOS-based) and hardware-token option for users prioritizing vendor independence, accepting increased complexity and support burden
review: Controls User Convenience vs. Vendor Independence. Weakness: The options do not adequately address the regulatory and certification burden for dual-path fallback; pursuing three authentication paths (MitID, mobile fallback, hardware fallback) may exceed eIDAS2 Substantial assurance requirements and face procurement rejection for complexity.

name: EU Standards Amplification and Timing
consequences: Immediate: Early EU engagement (phase two) on European Digital Identity Wallet framework allows project to seed platform-neutrality language into emerging standards, but requires specialized EU-facing resources and may create misalignment with Danish-specific AltID timelines. Systemic: Successful EU-level inclusion of platform-neutral requirements creates external leverage on Danish procurement and standards bodies, shifting debate from 'Danish preference' to 'EU compliance'; weak EU traction leaves project reliant solely on domestic procurement influence. Strategic: EU alignment can unlock co-funding or standards-body support, but Danish AltID decisions may proceed independently of EU wallet frameworks, limiting external pressure unless explicit regulatory linkage is established between the two.
options: Engage with EU eIDAS2 technical working groups and European Digital Identity Wallet governance during phase two, positioning platform-neutral and fallback-capable design as interoperability and portability requirements aligned with EU mandates
Defer EU engagement to phase three, allowing AltID and Danish procurement language to mature first, then use project outputs as evidence in EU-level consultation processes and standards reviews

Pursue parallel national and EU engagement: establish Danish coalition and procurement influence in phase two while simultaneously contributing technical evidence and standards language to EU wallet framework groups, accepting resource duplication but maximizing leverage on both levels
review: Controls Leverage Scope vs. Execution Focus. Weakness: The options assume EU wallet framework timelines align meaningfully with AltID procurement (months 17–24), but EU standards development may operate on a 2027–2029 timeline, leaving no feedback loop to influence the AltID window; the options do not specify contingency language for EU timing misalignment.

name: Technical Proof Anchor Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrator-first approach produces public-facing evidence by month 16, enabling media narrative and coalition pressure before AltID procurement hardens; production-grade certification delays all outputs 6+ months, missing the design-influence window. Systemic: Published demonstrators with external security audit establish 'proof-of-concept credibility' among technical audiences (EU standards bodies, university researchers, sympathetic procurement experts), while production certification adds regulatory authority but only after AltID decisions are likely finalized. Strategic: Demonstrator route risks dismissal as 'not production-ready' by conservative operators; production route guarantees regulatory standing but nearly guarantees AltID timeline miss and shifts project scope toward long-term standards influence rather than immediate procurement intervention.
options: Non-production demonstrators using GrapheneOS and open standards (FIDO2, OpenID Connect), published with independent security audit, positioned explicitly as feasibility evidence rather than product-ready software

Hybrid prototype seeking limited MitID operator technical collaboration to validate fallback authentication against real identity-provider infrastructure without formal certification, accepting 8–12 week review cycles and incremental institutional access

Production-grade eIDAS2 Substantial certification pathway, deferring all public deliverables 7+ months and committing to full regulatory compliance framework, guaranteeing legitimacy but forfeiting AltID design-stage influence
review: Controls Technical Credibility vs. Timeline Urgency. Weakness: The options do not address the scenario in which independent security audit itself becomes a bottleneck (external auditors may require 3–4 months lead time and charge 200k+ DKK); the options assume audit availability and speed without contingency for audit-queue delays or cost overruns that compress phase-two timeline.

name: Procurement Language Framing
consequences: Immediate: Resilience framing (supplier concentration, service-continuity, redundancy) appeals to IT operations and risk-management stakeholders, increasing support from within public-sector IT governance; sovereignty framing triggers nationalist sentiment among politicians but alienates pragmatists and may be weaponized as ideological rather than technical. Systemic: Resilience language positions platform-neutrality as insurance against Apple/Google policy changes, infrastructure outages, or geopolitical sanctions, shifting debate from sovereignty to business continuity; sovereignty language positions it as democratic accountability and strategic autonomy, but concentrates coalition among digital-independence advocates and may repel procurement officials. Strategic: Choice of framing determines which stakeholder groups champion the requirement in AltID specification documents; resilience framing wins support from contingency planners and security architects, while sovereignty framing wins political backing but may require separate technical legitimacy argument.
options: Lead with supplier-concentration and service-continuity risk narrative, positioning platform neutrality as essential redundancy and business-continuity insurance against Apple/Google policy shifts, infrastructure failure, or geopolitical sanctions, appealing to IT operations and risk-management stakeholders

Emphasize digital sovereignty, democratic accountability, and reduced foreign vendor influence, framing fallback access as a public-sector right and essential component of EU strategic autonomy, targeting political decision-makers and digital-independence advocates

Combine resilience and standards-alignment messaging, situating platform-neutral requirements within EU eIDAS2 interoperability, portability, and accessibility mandates, positioning fallback mechanisms as EU compliance measures rather than Danish preference, depoliticizing the argument
review: Controls Message Authority vs. Stakeholder Coalition. Weakness: The options assume message framing can be deployed sequentially or independently per stakeholder, but procurement committees typically require multiple overlapping justifications; the options do not specify which frame should be primary in official AltID documentation, creating risk of contradictory messaging that undermines coherence.

name: Institutional Engagement Sequencing
consequences: Immediate: Early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator during phase one (months 1–8) establishes dialogue and allows feasibility findings to shape AltID architecture at design stage, but risks premature rejection if technical evidence is incomplete; delayed engagement until phase two-three maximizes technical credibility but may encounter entrenched architectural decisions and require standards/EU pressure as alternative leverage. Systemic: Early engagement creates feedback loops allowing project to validate assumptions with operators and adjust approach iteratively; late engagement forces advocacy against fixed specifications and requires external (EU/standards-body) pressure to dislodge entrenched decisions. Strategic: Engagement sequencing directly controls whether the project influences AltID specification or merely comments on finalized procurement language; sequencing also determines resource allocation (early engagement may require diplomat-level policy staff; late engagement may rely more heavily on technical demonstrators and external credibility).
options: Prioritize formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID governance stakeholders during phase one using preliminary feasibility findings and coalition roadmap, accepting risk of early rejection but maximizing design-stage influence and iterative feedback loops

Sequence engagement conservatively: complete full technical demonstrators, independent security review, and external standards alignment in phase two before approaching Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and procurement bodies in phase three, deferring institutional dialogue until outputs are mature and dismissal risk is lower

Conduct parallel engagement strategy: low-visibility coalition building with sympathetic Folketinget members and procurement experts during phase one, formal technical engagement with MitID operator on fallback authentication architecture in early phase two, and official Digitaliseringsstyrelsen procurement engagement only in phase three once demonstrators and political coalition are established
review: Controls Influence Timing vs. Evidence Maturity. Weakness: None of the options address the scenario in which Digitaliseringsstyrelsen is actively hostile or indifferent to platform-neutrality requirements and prefers to extend the Apple/Google relationship indefinitely; the options assume receptiveness exists and do not specify alternative procurement levers (e.g., Folketinget budget amendment, EU regulatory pressure) if institutional goodwill is absent.

name: Fallback Authentication Technology Scope
consequences: Immediate: Mobile-centric fallback (GrapheneOS-based) maintains high usability and reduces citizen friction, but remains tethered to mobile OS ecosystems and does not eliminate platform dependence (merely replaces Apple/Google with open-source alternatives); browser-based fallback (FIDO2, WebAuthn, hardware tokens) achieves true OS independence but requires infrastructure investment and imposes UX burden on users accustomed to app-based convenience. Systemic: Mobile fallback duplicates the original problem at a smaller scale (open-source fork dependency replaces proprietary OS dependency) and faces sustainability risk if GrapheneOS cannot be maintained long-term; hardware-token fallback distributes trust across multiple independent authenticators but requires citizens to manage physical tokens and public-sector IT to support heterogeneous authentication devices. Strategic: Procurement bodies will prefer mobile option for citizen convenience and reduced infrastructure cost; technical purists will favor hardware-token option for true vendor independence; project credibility depends on demonstrating that one or both options satisfy eIDAS2 Substantial assurance level without requiring exclusive reliance on Apple/Google.
options: Develop mobile-first fallback using GrapheneOS or comparable privacy-respecting Android derivative, emphasizing open-source transparency and citizen ease-of-use, while acknowledging continued dependence on open-source mobile OS ecosystems and accepting long-term maintenance risk as trade-off for high usability

Design browser-based and hardware-token-centric fallback path (FIDO2/U2F, WebAuthn, open-source authenticator firmware), eliminating exclusive reliance on any single mobile OS vendor or platform provider, but requiring public-sector IT infrastructure modernization and citizen willingness to manage hardware authenticators

Create asymmetric dual-path fallback: GrapheneOS-based mobile option for mainstream citizens (high usability, moderate OS dependence), hardware-token option for government employees and security-critical populations (high independence, lower usability), accepting implementation complexity and support fragmentation as acceptable trade-off for differentiated assurance levels
review: Controls User Convenience vs. Vendor Independence. Weakness: The options do not adequately address whether eIDAS2 Substantial assurance level can be achieved using hardware tokens alone without mandatory mobile OS mediation; if EU certification requires mobile app as primary path with hardware as fallback only, then hardware-token-first or dual-path options may be regulatory non-starters and reduce option viability to mobile-only.

name: EU Standards Amplification and Timing
consequences: Immediate: Early EU engagement (phase two) on European Digital Identity Wallet framework allows project to seed platform-neutrality language into emerging EU standards, creating external regulatory pressure on Danish AltID procurement, but requires specialized EU-facing resources and Danish project may be perceived as imposing local constraints onto EU-wide standards; late EU engagement (phase three) allows Danish outcomes to inform EU input, but misses opportunity to influence wallet architecture if EU timeline accelerates. Systemic: Successful EU-level inclusion of platform-neutral requirements creates formal regulatory lever on Danish procurement and standards bodies, shifting debate from 'Danish preference' to 'EU compliance mandate'; weak EU traction leaves project dependent solely on domestic coalition and procurement influence, which may be insufficient if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen prioritizes convenience over sovereignty. Strategic: EU alignment can unlock co-funding, standards-body legitimacy, and multilateral pressure; Danish AltID decisions may proceed independently of EU wallet frameworks (scheduled for different timelines), creating risk that EU influence arrives too late to affect domestic procurement, unless explicit regulatory linkage is established beforehand.
options: Engage with EU eIDAS2 technical working groups and European Digital Identity Wallet governance during phase two, positioning platform-neutral and fallback-capable design as interoperability, portability, and accessibility requirements aligned with EU mandates, aiming to embed platform-neutrality language into formal EU specifications by month 16

Defer EU engagement to phase three, allowing AltID and Danish procurement language to mature independently first, then use project outputs (demonstrators, feasibility report, Danish policy language) as evidence in EU-level consultation processes and standards reviews, accepting that EU influence may arrive too late for AltID but contributing to long-term wallet framework

Pursue parallel national and EU engagement with explicit timeline coordination: establish Danish coalition and early Digitaliseringsstyrelsen contact in phase one, present technical demonstrators and Danish procurement language to EU working groups in phase two (months 9–12), then use EU feedback and emerging wallet standards to reinforce Danish phase-three procurement language, accepting resource duplication and diplomatic complexity but maximizing bidirectional influence
review: Controls Leverage Scope vs. Execution Focus. Weakness: The options assume European Digital Identity Wallet governance timelines are knowable and controllable from Danish project perspective, but EU standards development may operate on a 2027–2029 timeline independent of AltID schedule (months 9–24); the options do not specify contingency language for fundamental timeline misalignment or procedures for pivoting Danish input if EU working-group schedule does not intersect meaningfully with AltID procurement window.

name: Technical Proof Anchor Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrator-first approach produces public-facing evidence by month 16, enabling media narrative and coalition pressure before AltID procurement hardens; production-grade certification delays all outputs 6+ months, missing the design-influence window. Systemic: Published demonstrators with external security audit establish 'proof-of-concept credibility' among technical audiences (EU standards bodies, university researchers, sympathetic procurement experts), while production certification adds regulatory authority but only after AltID decisions are likely finalized. Strategic: Demonstrator route risks dismissal as 'not production-ready' by conservative operators; production route guarantees regulatory standing but nearly guarantees AltID timeline miss and shifts project scope toward long-term standards influence rather than immediate procurement intervention.
options: Non-production demonstrators using GrapheneOS and open standards (FIDO2, OpenID Connect), published with independent security audit, positioned explicitly as feasibility evidence rather than product-ready software, releasing findings through academic conference and policy channels by month 15

Hybrid prototype seeking limited MitID operator technical collaboration to validate fallback authentication against real identity-provider infrastructure without formal certification, accepting 8–12 week operator review cycles and incremental institutional access in exchange for credibility with incumbent operators

Production-grade eIDAS2 Substantial certification pathway, deferring all public deliverables 7+ months and committing to full regulatory compliance framework, guaranteeing legitimacy but forfeiting AltID design-stage influence and shifting success criteria toward long-term European standards and future procurement cycles
review: Controls Technical Credibility vs. Timeline Urgency. Weakness: The options do not address the scenario in which independent security audit itself becomes a bottleneck or cost overrun (external auditors may require 3–4 months lead time, charge 200k+ DKK, and demand audit rights that constrain demonstrator release timing); the options assume audit availability and speed without contingency budget or escalation procedures if audit delays compress phase-two timeline below 4 months of integration and testing.

name: Procurement Language Framing
consequences: Immediate: Resilience framing (supplier concentration, service-continuity, redundancy) appeals to IT operations and risk-management stakeholders, increasing support from within public-sector IT governance and Statsministeriet's continuity planning; sovereignty framing triggers nationalist sentiment among politicians but alienates pragmatists and may be weaponized as ideological rather than technical by incumbent operators defending Apple/Google relationships. Systemic: Resilience language positions platform-neutrality as insurance against Apple/Google policy changes, infrastructure outages, or geopolitical sanctions, shifting debate from sovereignty to business continuity; sovereignty language positions it as democratic accountability and strategic autonomy, but concentrates coalition among digital-independence advocates and may repel procurement officials who fear political perception of 'anti-American' positioning. Strategic: Choice of framing determines which stakeholder groups champion the requirement in AltID specification documents; resilience framing wins support from contingency planners, security architects, and Forsvarets Cyberagenturs threat-assessment perspective, while sovereignty framing wins political backing from specific Folketinget members but may require separate technical legitimacy argument to survive procurement scrutiny.
options: Lead with supplier-concentration and service-continuity risk narrative, positioning platform neutrality as essential redundancy and business-continuity insurance against Apple/Google policy shifts, infrastructure failure, or geopolitical sanctions, appealing directly to IT operations, risk-management stakeholders, and continuity planners in Statsministeriet and public-sector IT agencies

Emphasize digital sovereignty, democratic accountability, and reduced foreign vendor influence, framing fallback access as a public-sector right, constitutional data protection, and essential component of EU strategic autonomy, targeting political decision-makers, digital-independence advocates, and sympathetic Folketinget members who prioritize long-term independence over short-term convenience

Combine resilience and standards-alignment messaging, situating platform-neutral requirements within EU eIDAS2 interoperability, portability, accessibility, and non-discrimination mandates, positioning fallback mechanisms as EU compliance measures and interoperability necessities rather than Danish nationalist preference, depoliticizing the argument and appealing to procurement pragmatists focused on EU regulatory alignment
review: Controls Message Authority vs. Stakeholder Coalition. Weakness: The options assume message framing can be deployed sequentially or independently per stakeholder, but procurement committees typically require multiple overlapping justifications; none of the options specify which frame should be primary in official AltID documentation, or how contradictory messaging across different stakeholder channels (e.g., resilience to IT operations, sovereignty to politicians, compliance to procurement) will be reconciled into a coherent procurement requirement.

name: Institutional Engagement Sequencing
consequences: Immediate: Early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator during phase one (months 1–8) establishes dialogue and allows feasibility findings to shape AltID architecture at design stage, but risks premature rejection and entry into 'ideas already considered and dismissed' category; delayed engagement until phase two-three maximizes technical credibility but may encounter entrenched architectural decisions and require standards/EU pressure as alternative leverage since formal opposition will have been published. Systemic: Early engagement creates feedback loops allowing project to validate assumptions with operators and adjust approach iteratively; late engagement forces advocacy against fixed specifications and requires external (EU/standards-body) pressure to dislodge entrenched decisions or necessitates political intervention through Folketinget budget amendments. Strategic: Engagement sequencing directly controls whether the project influences AltID specification or merely comments on finalized procurement language; sequencing also determines resource allocation (early engagement requires experienced policy staff and relationship capital; late engagement relies more heavily on technical demonstrators, external credibility, and political leverage).
options: Prioritize formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID governance stakeholders during phase one using preliminary feasibility findings and coalition roadmap, accepting risk of early rejection but establishing dialogue loop and maximizing design-stage influence; if rejected, shift to EU standards input and long-term coalition consolidation

Sequence engagement conservatively: complete full technical demonstrators, independent security review, and external standards alignment in phase two before approaching Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and procurement bodies in phase three, deferring institutional dialogue until outputs are mature and dismissal risk is lower, but accepting that AltID specifications may be finalized before phase-three engagement window opens

Conduct asymmetric parallel engagement: low-visibility coalition building with sympathetic Folketinget members, academic researchers, and contingency-planning stakeholders during phase one; technical engagement with MitID operator on fallback authentication architecture in early phase two (months 9–11), limited to architectural validation without formal procurement advocacy; official Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID procurement engagement only in phase three (months 17–20) once demonstrators, security audit, and political coalition are established, allowing 4-month window before AltID finalization
review: Controls Influence Timing vs. Evidence Maturity. Weakness: None of the options address the scenario in which Digitaliseringsstyrelsen is actively hostile to platform-neutrality requirements and prefers indefinite Apple/Google relationship; the options assume receptiveness exists and do not specify alternative procurement levers (e.g., Folketinget budget amendment linking AltID funding to platform-neutrality requirement, media campaign exposing supplier concentration risk, EU regulatory escalation) if institutional goodwill is absent or if phase-one engagement produces formal rejection.

name: Fallback Authentication Technology Scope
consequences: Immediate: Mobile-centric fallback (GrapheneOS-based) maintains high usability and reduces citizen friction, but remains tethered to mobile OS ecosystems and does not eliminate platform dependence (merely replaces Apple/Google with open-source alternatives); browser-based fallback (FIDO2, WebAuthn, hardware tokens) achieves true OS independence but requires public-sector IT infrastructure investment and imposes UX burden on users accustomed to app-based convenience. Systemic: Mobile fallback duplicates the original problem at a smaller scale (open-source fork dependency replaces proprietary OS dependency) and faces sustainability risk if GrapheneOS cannot be maintained long-term without external Android source updates; hardware-token fallback distributes trust across multiple independent authenticators but requires citizens to manage physical tokens and creates IT support burden for heterogeneous device classes. Strategic: Procurement bodies will prefer mobile option for citizen convenience and reduced infrastructure cost; technical purists will favor hardware-token option for true vendor independence; project must demonstrate that chosen option satisfies eIDAS2 Substantial assurance level without requiring exclusive reliance on Apple/Google while remaining cost-justified against incumbent MitID maintenance expenses.
options: Develop mobile-first fallback using GrapheneOS or comparable privacy-respecting Android derivative, emphasizing open-source transparency and citizen ease-of-use, while explicitly acknowledging continued dependence on open-source mobile OS ecosystem and documenting long-term maintenance risk and GrapheneOS sustainability assumptions; position as pragmatic compromise between convenience and sovereignty

Design browser-based and hardware-token-centric fallback path (FIDO2/U2F, WebAuthn, open-source authenticator firmware, optional NFC-based hardware tokens), eliminating exclusive reliance on any single mobile OS vendor, but requiring public-sector IT infrastructure modernization and citizen willingness to manage hardware authenticators; position as highest-assurance option for security-critical populations (government employees, public administrators)

Create asymmetric dual-path fallback: GrapheneOS-based mobile option for mainstream citizens (high usability, moderate OS independence), hardware-token option for government employees and security-critical populations (high independence, lower usability), with explicit cost-benefit analysis documenting support fragmentation, training burden, and assurance-level differentiation as justifiable trade-offs for serving heterogeneous citizen populations with differentiated trust requirements
review: Controls User Convenience vs. Vendor Independence. Weakness: The options do not adequately address whether eIDAS2 Substantial assurance level can be achieved using hardware tokens alone without mandatory mobile OS mediation; if EU certification or Danish regulatory interpretation requires mobile app as primary path with hardware as fallback only, then hardware-token-first or dual-path options may be regulatory non-starters; the options also do not specify what happens if GrapheneOS community abandons the project mid-implementation or if Google terminates Android source-code access that GrapheneOS depends on.

name: EU Standards Amplification and Timing
consequences: Immediate: Early EU engagement (phase two, months 9–12) on European Digital Identity Wallet framework allows project to seed platform-neutrality language into emerging EU standards, creating external regulatory pressure on Danish AltID procurement; late EU engagement (phase three) allows Danish outcomes to inform EU input, but misses opportunity to influence wallet architecture if EU standards timeline accelerates and wallet governance decisions are finalized before month 17. Systemic: Successful EU-level inclusion of platform-neutral requirements creates formal regulatory lever on Danish procurement, shifting debate from 'Danish preference' to 'EU compliance mandate'; weak EU traction leaves project dependent solely on domestic coalition and procurement influence, which may be insufficient if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen prioritizes convenience and cost-minimization over sovereignty and vendor independence. Strategic: EU alignment can unlock co-funding, standards-body legitimacy, and multilateral pressure on platform vendors to support open standards; Danish AltID decisions may proceed independently of EU wallet frameworks (scheduled for different timelines), creating risk that EU influence arrives too late to affect domestic procurement, unless explicit regulatory linkage is established beforehand through eIDAS2 compliance language or member-state coordination mechanisms.
options: Engage with EU eIDAS2 technical working groups and European Digital Identity Wallet governance during phase two (months 9–12), positioning platform-neutral and fallback-capable design as interoperability, portability, accessibility, and non-discrimination requirements aligned with EU mandates, aiming to embed platform-neutrality language into formal EU specifications and wallet-architecture documents by month 15, creating external regulatory pressure on Danish AltID procurement before phase-three engagement begins

Defer EU engagement to phase three (months 17–20), allowing AltID and Danish procurement language to mature independently first, then use project outputs (demonstrators, feasibility report, Danish policy language) as evidence in EU-level consultation processes, standards reviews, and member-state forums, accepting that EU influence may arrive too late for AltID but contributing to long-term wallet framework and creating precedent for future European member-state digital identity procurement

Pursue parallel national and EU engagement with explicit timeline coordination: establish Danish coalition and early Digitaliseringsstyrelsen contact in phase one, present technical demonstrators and Danish procurement language to EU working groups in phase two (months 9–12), then use EU feedback and emerging wallet standards to reinforce Danish phase-three procurement language, accepting resource duplication and diplomatic complexity but maximizing bidirectional influence and creating regulatory lock-in at both national and European levels
review: Controls Leverage Scope vs. Execution Focus. Weakness: The options assume European Digital Identity Wallet governance timelines are knowable and controllable from Danish project perspective, but EU standards development may operate on a 2027–2029 timeline independent of AltID schedule; the options do not specify contingency language for fundamental timeline misalignment, procedures for pivoting Danish input if EU working-group schedule does not intersect meaningfully with AltID procurement window, or mechanisms for leveraging EU compliance language if wallet standards finalize after AltID procurement decisions are already made.
